title: Hormuz Reopening Looms as Trump Declares Naval Blockade Lift
subhead: US-Iran negotiations approach a potential breakthrough on Strait of Hormuz access, while Ukraine escalates strikes on Russian oil infrastructure and UAE's secret role in the Iran conflict reshapes Gulf energy politics.

sections.heading: Strait of Hormuz: Blockade Lift Signals Potential Ceasefire Extension
sections.body:
President Donald Trump announced he is lifting the US naval blockade of the Strait of Hormuz, declaring on Truth Social that 'Ships caught in the Strait due to our amazing and unprecedented Naval Blockade, which will now be lifted, may start the process of heading home!' The move comes as US and Iranian negotiators work toward a 60-day ceasefire extension that would allow formal negotiations on Iran's nuclear program.

According to the BBC and Al Jazeera, Trump convened a Situation Room meeting on Friday to make a 'final determination' on the framework agreement. Iranian state media outlet Fars reported the agreement is in its 'final stages of ratification,' though Iran's foreign ministry emphasized that 'no final agreement' has been reached.

The tentative framework requires Iran to never possess a nuclear weapon, reopen Hormuz for unrestricted shipping traffic in both directions, and allow the US to 'unearth and destroy' buried enriched uranium. Trump stated that 'no money will be exchanged until further notice.' Iran's chief negotiator Mohammad Bagher Ghalibaf signaled deep mistrust: 'No action will be taken before the other side acts... The winner of any agreement is the one who is better prepared for war the day after.'

The Strait of Hormuz passage is critical: approximately 20% of global energy supplies normally transit through the waterway. Its closure since the US-Iran conflict began in late February has contributed to elevated global oil prices. Iranian negotiators pushed back on Trump's characterization, with foreign ministry spokesperson Esmaeil Baghaei stating that the US blockade was 'illegal from the beginning' and that Tehran would judge any agreement by actions, not words.
sections.whyItMatters: A Hormuz reopening would ease pressure on global oil markets and reduce immediate escalation risk, but the deep distrust between Washington and Tehran—combined with unresolved nuclear issues—means any ceasefire remains fragile. Iran's insistence on action-for-action sequencing creates significant implementation risk.

sections.heading: UAE's Secret Air Campaign Against Iranian Energy Infrastructure
sections.body:
The Wall Street Journal revealed that the United Arab Emirates conducted dozens of airstrikes against Iran during the three-month conflict, coordinating with US and Israeli intelligence to target Iranian energy infrastructure. According to the Jerusalem Post summary of the WSJ report, Iran fired over 2,800 missiles and drones at the UAE—more than at any other country, including Israel—prompting Abu Dhabi to abandon its initial stance of neutrality.

UAE strikes targeted Iranian energy facilities including the Asaluyeh petrochemical complex, installations on Qeshm and Abu Musa islands in the Strait of Hormuz, Bandar Abbas port, and an oil refinery on Lavan island in the Persian Gulf. The Asaluyeh strike was conducted jointly with Israel and drew international criticism, including a request from Trump for Israel to stop striking energy facilities.

The revelations help explain the UAE's April withdrawal from OPEC and OPEC+—a dramatic break from the oil cartel that Bloomberg and WSJ linked to Emirati frustration with Gulf neighbors who 'refused to coordinate a military response' to Iranian attacks. UAE President Mohammed bin Zayed reportedly expressed frustration with Saudi Arabia and Qatar during phone calls with Saudi Crown Prince Mohammed bin Salman, according to sources cited by Bloomberg.

Israel significantly deepened its security ties with the UAE during the conflict, deploying Iron Dome batteries and IDF soldiers to Emirati territory. Multiple high-level Israeli officials visited Abu Dhabi, including Mossad Director David Barnea, Shin Bet Director David Zini, IDF Chief of Staff Eyal Zamir, and Prime Minister Netanyahu.
sections.whyItMatters: The UAE's covert air campaign and OPEC withdrawal mark a fundamental realignment in Gulf energy politics. Abu Dhabi's willingness to strike Iranian energy infrastructure—and to coordinate with Israel on joint operations—signals that the Gulf's traditional security architecture has fragmented, with the UAE pursuing independent military and energy strategies that diverge sharply from Saudi Arabia's more cautious approach.

sections.heading: Ukraine Escalates Strikes on Russian Oil Infrastructure
sections.body:
Ukrainian forces conducted coordinated strikes on oil infrastructure across Russian and Russian-held territories overnight on May 30, according to Russian Telegram channels cited by the Kyiv Independent. The attacks targeted fuel storage and transport facilities in Taganrog—a major port city on the Sea of Azov in Rostov Oblast—and Feodosia in occupied Crimea, where residents reported a burning oil depot.

In Taganrog, Rostov Oblast Governor Yuri Slusar confirmed that a fuel tank, oil tanker, and administrative building in the port caught fire following the drone strike, with two people injured. The city lies just 40 kilometers from the Ukrainian border and has been frequently targeted in Ukraine's campaign against Russian oil infrastructure.

According to data compiled by the Poland-based outlet Vot Tak, Ukraine has struck Russian oil refineries at least 158 times since the full-scale invasion began. Ukraine has increasingly relied on domestically produced drones to target facilities tied to oil refining, logistics, and defense production deep inside Russian territory.

Earlier in May, Ukraine struck the Novorossiysk oil depot and offloading terminal on the Black Sea and the Tamanneftegaz terminal, as well as industrial sites 1,700 kilometers inside Russia, including the Metafrax Chemicals plant in Perm. The sustained campaign against Russian oil infrastructure serves dual purposes: degrading Russian logistics and striking at a key source of war financing.
sections.whyItMatters: Ukraine's systematic targeting of Russian oil infrastructure demonstrates continued capability to project power deep into Russian territory while striking at Moscow's primary revenue source. The rate of Russian casualties—145,000 in 2026 alone, nearly triple last year's rate according to Ukrainian officials—combined with the oil infrastructure losses, creates compounding pressure on Russian war financing.

sections.heading: Russia Sells 27.9 Tonnes of Gold as War Strains Economy
sections.body:
Russia has sold 27.9 tonnes of gold reserves so far in 2026, worth more than $4 billion, according to Central Bank of Russia data cited by Al Jazeera. The sales leave Russian gold reserves at their lowest level since February 2022—the start of the full-scale invasion of Ukraine.

The gold sales come as Russia exceeded its entire 2026 budget deficit allowance by April, according to Ukrainian Defence Minister Mykhailo Fedorov, and has 'gutted its foreign exchange reserves.' The rate of Russian losses—179 casualties per square kilometer of advance compared to 67 last year—exceeds current replacement capacity through recruitment.

The combination of territorial pressure, elevated casualties, and gold reserve depletion signals mounting economic strain. Russia has also faced Ukrainian territorial gains—Ukraine retook approximately 400 square kilometers around Dnipropetrovsk in the first quarter of 2026, according to the US Defence Intelligence Agency, the most territory recovered since late 2022.
sections.whyItMatters: Russia's accelerated gold sales and budget deficit breach reveal the financial toll of sustained high-intensity warfare. The gold drawdown is a visible indicator that sanctions and military expenditure are depleting reserves faster than the Kremlin's public statements suggest—though Russia retains significant reserves and alternative revenue mechanisms.

sections.heading: Russian Drone Strike on NATO Territory Tests Alliance
sections.body:
A Russian Geran-2 drone struck a residential building in the Romanian city of Galati overnight, injuring two people and prompting NATO condemnation. The drone was part of a swarm of 43 Russian drones attacking Ukraine; only one veered into Romanian territory, according to Romanian President Nicusor Dan. The drone was carrying at least 30 kilograms of explosives when it hit the apartment building near the Ukrainian border.

Romania called it the 'worst incident to hit national territory' since the war began, expelled the Russian consul, and ordered the consulate in Constanta closed. NATO Secretary-General Mark Rutte expressed 'absolute solidarity' with Romania and said the alliance 'stands ready to defend every inch of Allied territory.'

The incident adds to a pattern of airspace violations across NATO's eastern flank. Ukrainian drones have also struck targets in Estonia and Latvia, and were shot down by Romanian fighter jets stationed in Lithuania. A senior US military official told reporters that Russian 'hybrid activities'—drone incursions, hacking attacks, and other acts short of military force—have increased in recent years as part of a campaign to achieve strategic objectives without triggering war.

Russian President Vladimir Putin said the drone's origin is 'yet to be determined' and urged Romania to turn it over for 'objective investigation.' Deputy Security Council Chairman Dmitry Medvedev told European leaders to 'just shut up' about the incident, calling them 'scoundrels' and 'imbeciles.'
sections.whyItMatters: While NATO has stopped short of invoking Article 5, the Galati incident highlights the persistent risk of escalation from the Ukraine war's spillover effects. Romania's diplomatic response—expelling diplomats rather than seeking military retaliation—reflects the alliance's calibrated approach to Russian provocations on NATO territory.

sections.heading: EU Tightens Trade Defenses Against China Over Industrial Overcapacity
sections.body:
The European Commission agreed on a tougher trade policy toward China on Friday, with trade chief Maros Sefcovic outlining measures to counter what is described as a new 'China shock' to European industries. The EU's six largest economies backed stronger trade defense measures, according to the South China Morning Post.

The new approach may include developing an instrument to compel companies in critical sectors to diversify suppliers—both in number and geographic location. Sefcovic is expected to ramp up safeguard measures in sectors under pressure from Chinese overcapacity, particularly chemicals and machinery. Safeguards are faster and harder for dissenting member states to block, requiring only a qualified majority to obstruct.

Beijing vowed retaliation. The policy shift marks an intensification of EU-China trade tensions, with Brussels warning of the threat to European industrial capacity from subsidized Chinese imports. Commission President Ursula von der Leyen is supportive of the more robust approach.

This trade-focused debate is seen internally as 'the firing of a starting gun' ahead of an intense period for China policy, according to SCMP sources. The measures could significantly impact supply chains for critical minerals, renewable energy components, and advanced manufacturing inputs.
sections.whyItMatters: The EU's move signals that European industrial policy is shifting from economic cooperation toward strategic decoupling in critical sectors. For energy supply chains—particularly minerals processing, battery components, and clean technology—the new trade defense tools could accelerate supply chain reorganization and increase costs for European manufacturers.

bottomLine: The prospective Hormuz reopening represents the day's most significant energy development—with 20% of global energy supplies potentially flowing again—but the US-Iran framework remains fragile and contingent on mutual verification. The UAE's revelation as a major combatant against Iranian energy infrastructure, combined with its OPEC exit, reshapes Gulf energy politics fundamentally. Ukraine's continued strikes on Russian oil facilities demonstrate sustained deep-strike capability while Russia's $4 billion gold sale signals intensifying financial strain. Meanwhile, EU trade policy hardening against China will ripple through energy transition supply chains.
sources: Al Jazeera, BBC, Kyiv Independent, Jerusalem Post (citing Wall Street Journal), South China Morning Post, AP News
